taskDescription: Drag the correct anatomical terms into the blanks to complete statements about spinal cord anatomy.
textField:
The spinal cord extends from the foramen magnum to the level of the *L1* vertebra in adults, and its terminal end is called the *conus* medullaris. Below this, the filum terminale extends to the *coccyx*. The cauda equina consists of the spinal nerve roots descending below the conus medullaris within the *lumbar* cistern.

The *cervical* enlargement (C4-T1) supplies the upper limb via the brachial plexus, while the *lumbosacral* enlargement (L2-S3) supplies the lower limb. The spinal cord has *31* pairs of spinal nerves: 8 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral, and 1 coccygeal.

In *Brown-Sequard* syndrome (hemisection of the spinal cord), there is ipsilateral loss of motor function and *proprioception* (dorsal column damage) with contralateral loss of *pain* and temperature (spinothalamic tract damage). The contralateral sensory loss begins one or two segments below the *lesion*.

The lateral horn is present only at *T1-L2* levels and contains *preganglionic* sympathetic neurons. The sacral parasympathetic outflow arises from *S2-S4* segments. The dorsal columns carry *fine* touch, vibration, and proprioception, while the lateral *spinothalamic* tract carries pain and temperature.
